findings.rationale: The text is a complete German sentence ('Bei Fragen können Sie sich gerne an unsere Mitarbeiter im Kundenservice wenden.') containing substantive content and meaningful phrases. The page has no declared primary language, so the absence of a [lang] attribute on this German-language paragraph is a failure of SC 3.1.2. findings.outerHTML: Hier ist leider ein Fehler aufgetreten. Wir sind in Kürze wieder für Sie da.

evidence.textContent: Hier ist leider ein Fehler aufgetreten. Wir sind in Kürze wieder für Sie da.

findings.rationale: The page has no declared primary language (<html lang> is absent), making it impossible to definitively determine whether this German text represents a 'different language' from the page's primary language. The text itself is clearly German ('Hier ist leider ein Fehler aufgetreten. Wir sind in Kürze wieder für Sie da.' = 'An error has unfortunately occurred. We will be back for you shortly.'), and it is substantive content (a complete sentence conveying meaning). However, without a declared pageLang, the comparison required by MODE B cannot be reliably performed. If the page's intended primary language is German, this would be a pass; if it is any other language, this would be a fail.

The element contains 'USA' (a proper name/acronym), a phone number (language-neutral), and the German word 'gebührenfrei' (meaning 'toll-free'). Since the page has no declared primary language, it is unclear whether German is the expected language context. If the page is primarily German, 'gebührenfrei' is a loanword/common term in German contexts and would pass. If the page is primarily English or another language, 'gebührenfrei' would be a substantive German word requiring annotation. The ambiguity about page language intent prevents a confident pass or fail verdict.

The text contains 'Großbritannien' (a German proper noun for the place name 'Great Britain'), a phone number (+44 114 697 1778), and a country code. Place names are explicitly exempted from SC 3.1.2 annotation requirements per the normative exceptions. The phone number is language-neutral technical content. No substantive foreign-language phrase or sentence is present.

The text 'International +49 30 837 91488' contains a label ('International'), a country code (+49 for Germany), and a phone number. These are language-neutral elements: the word 'International' is a loanword/common English term that has entered many languages' vernacular, and phone numbers with country codes are not substantive foreign-language content. No [lang] annotation is required.

The text 'Deutschland +49 30 837 91488' contains a proper name (Deutschland, the German name for Germany) and a phone number with country code. A place name is explicitly exempt under SC 3.1.2 normative exceptions, and a phone number is language-neutral technical content. No substantive foreign-language phrase or sentence is present.

The text 'Frankreich +33 3 67 21 01 19' contains a proper name (Frankreich, the German/Dutch name for France) and a phone number. Proper names are explicitly exempted from SC 3.1.2 requirements. The phone number is language-neutral technical content. No substantive foreign-language phrase or sentence is present.

The text 'Australia +61 3 6413 0103' consists of a proper place name ('Australia') and a telephone number with country code. Proper names are explicitly exempted from SC 3.1.2 requirements. The phone number is language-neutral technical content. No substantive foreign-language phrase or sentence is present.
